questions.question: Which tenet of airpower states that airpower should be controlled by a single airman who maintains broad focus over the entire operational area while allowing subordinate commanders authority to respond to local conditions?
options: Centralized control and decentralized execution
Flexibility and versatility
Synergistic effects
Concentration

questions.question: Which tenet of airpower refers to the ability to employ airpower effectively at the strategic, operational, and tactical levels of warfare and to shift between them seamlessly?
options: Persistence
Flexibility and versatility
Balance
Priority

questions.question: The tenet of airpower that describes the integration of complementary air and space capabilities to produce effects greater than the sum of individual actions is known as what?
options: Concentration
Balance
Synergistic effects
Persistence

questions.question: Which tenet of airpower emphasizes the ability to sustain operations at a tempo and duration necessary to achieve desired effects?
options: Priority
Concentration
Flexibility and versatility
Persistence

questions.question: The tenet of 'concentration' in airpower refers to which of the following?
options: Massing overwhelming combat power at the decisive time and place to achieve decisive results
Sustaining operations for extended periods
Distributing forces evenly across the theater
Integrating air and ground forces at the tactical level

questions.question: Which tenet of airpower addresses the need to allocate airpower resources according to the relative importance of assigned tasks and objectives?
options: Concentration
Priority
Balance
Synergistic effects

questions.question: The tenet of 'balance' in airpower requires commanders to do what?
options: Always assign equal resources to offensive and defensive operations
Restrict operations to a single level of warfare
Maintain the proper mix of airpower capabilities to achieve synergistic effects across the range of operations
Focus exclusively on the most immediate threat

questions.question: Which Air Force core mission involves gaining and maintaining control of the air domain to permit friendly air, ground, and maritime operations?
options: Global strike
Rapid global mobility
Command and control
Air superiority

questions.question: Which Air Force core mission provides the ability to rapidly project power and strike any target on the globe?
options: Global strike
Air superiority
Rapid global mobility
Intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance

questions.question: Rapid global mobility as an Air Force core mission encompasses which of the following capabilities?
options: Counterair and counterspace operations
Airlift, air refueling, and aeromedical evacuation
Strategic attack and interdiction
Defensive counterair and offensive counterair

questions.question: Which Air Force core mission involves collecting, processing, exploiting, and disseminating data to provide timely information to commanders?
options: Air superiority
Command and control
Intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance (ISR)
Global strike

questions.question: The Air Force core mission of command and control (C2) is best described as which of the following?
options: Developing training programs for aircrews
Procuring and sustaining weapon systems
Conducting defensive cyberspace operations
The exercise of authority and direction by a commander over assigned and attached forces to accomplish the mission

questions.question: Counterair operations encompass both offensive counterair (OCA) and defensive counterair (DCA). What is the primary purpose of OCA?
options: Destroying or disrupting enemy air and missile capabilities at their source before they can be employed
Detecting and intercepting enemy aircraft after they are airborne
Providing air refueling to friendly fighters
Conducting surveillance of enemy ground forces

questions.question: Defensive counterair (DCA) operations are designed to accomplish which objective?
options: Attacking enemy airfields and missile sites
Detecting, identifying, intercepting, and destroying enemy air and missile threats attempting to attack friendly forces or penetrate friendly airspace
Providing strategic airlift to deploy ground forces
Conducting intelligence collection on enemy air defenses

questions.question: Counterspace operations are designed to achieve what primary effect?
options: Establishing permanent military bases on the moon
Increasing the range of strategic bombers
Space superiority by ensuring freedom of action in space for friendly forces while denying the same to the adversary
Deploying ground-based missile defense systems

questions.question: Counterland operations include which two broad categories?
options: Counterair and countersea
Airlift and air refueling
Space control and space force enhancement
Air interdiction (AI) and close air support (CAS)

questions.question: Air interdiction (AI) operations are conducted against enemy targets at what relative location?
options: Targets at sufficient distance from friendly surface forces that detailed integration of each air mission with the fire and movement of those forces is not required
Targets in direct contact with friendly ground forces
Targets within the boundaries of the continental United States
Targets exclusively within enemy airspace

questions.question: Close air support (CAS) is defined as air action against hostile targets that are in close proximity to friendly forces and requires what key element?
options: Strategic-level planning from the JFACC
Detailed integration with the fire and movement of friendly ground forces
Employment of only precision-guided munitions
Authorization from the National Command Authority

questions.question: Which air and space power function involves the use of air operations against maritime threats to gain and maintain the desired degree of control of sea lines of communication?
options: Strategic attack
Counterland
Countersea
Counterspace

questions.question: Strategic attack targets an adversary's key centers of gravity and is intended to achieve what kind of effect?
options: Exclusively tactical effects on the front lines
Limited effects confined to a single engagement area
Only psychological effects on the civilian population
Strategic or operational-level effects that directly support joint force commander objectives

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the air tasking order (ATO)?
options: Translating the joint force commander's air apportionment decision into specific air missions and sorties to achieve objectives
Assigning individual airmen to specific aircraft
Providing maintenance schedules for all aircraft in the theater
Establishing rules of engagement for ground forces

questions.question: The air tasking order (ATO) cycle typically covers what time period?
options: One week
24 hours
72 hours
One month

questions.question: Who is the designated commander responsible for recommending the apportionment of joint air capabilities and forces, planning and coordinating joint air operations, and tasking those operations?
options: The joint force land component commander
The combatant commander
The joint force air component commander (JFACC)
The chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff

questions.question: In joint operations, who normally designates the JFACC?
options: The Secretary of Defense
The chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff
The air component commander
The joint force commander (JFC)

questions.question: The theater air control system (TACS) provides the JFACC the ability to accomplish what primary function?
options: Command, control, and coordination of air operations within the theater
Procurement of new aircraft for the theater
Training of all joint forces in the area of operations
Direct management of all ground force maneuver elements

questions.question: Which element of the theater air control system serves as the senior element of the TACS and is the war-fighting headquarters through which the JFACC plans, coordinates, allocates, and tasks joint air operations?
options: The control and reporting center (CRC)
The air operations center (AOC)
The tactical air control party (TACP)
The direct air support center (DASC)

questions.question: What is the purpose of the air refueling function within air and space power?
options: Transporting fuel to forward-deployed ground units
Refueling naval vessels at sea
Extending the range, payload, and endurance of receiver aircraft, enabling global reach and operational flexibility
Providing fuel storage at deployed locations

questions.question: Aeromedical evacuation is the movement of patients to and between medical treatment facilities using what means?
options: Ground ambulances under Air Force control
Hospital ships coordinated by the Air Force
Civilian contract aircraft exclusively
Fixed-wing or rotary-wing aircraft with in-flight medical care

questions.question: Agile combat support encompasses which broad set of capabilities?
options: The core logistics and support functions that enable airpower generation, sustainment, and the projection of combat capability
Only aircraft maintenance and repair operations
Exclusively financial management and contracting services
Intelligence analysis and targeting functions

questions.question: Airlift as a function of air and space power includes which of the following?
options: Only intertheater airlift between continents
Both intertheater (strategic) and intratheater (tactical) airlift of personnel, equipment, and supplies
Air-to-air refueling of transport aircraft
Only intratheater movement within a single area of operations

questions.question: What is Agile Combat Employment (ACE)?
options: A maintenance concept for rapidly repairing battle-damaged aircraft
A logistics system for tracking spare parts globally
A proactive and reactive operational scheme of maneuver that increases survivability by leveraging a network of distributed operating locations to generate combat airpower
A training program designed to certify multi-capable airmen

questions.question: A key concept of Agile Combat Employment (ACE) is the use of what to complicate adversary targeting and increase force survivability?
options: Large, consolidated main operating bases exclusively
Permanent fixed infrastructure at all locations
Reduced communication between deployed units
A network of smaller, dispersed forward operating locations rather than concentrating forces at a few large bases

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the concept of 'centralized control' within the tenet of centralized control and decentralized execution?
options: A single commander maintains the broad, strategic perspective needed to synchronize operations, prioritize efforts, and ensure unity of purpose across the force
Every tactical decision must be approved by the theater commander before execution
All air assets must be physically located at one central airfield
Only the President can authorize the employment of airpower

questions.question: In the context of 'decentralized execution,' what authority is delegated to subordinate commanders?
options: The authority to set overall theater strategy
The authority to make tactical-level decisions and adapt to local conditions while operating within the commander's intent
The authority to bypass the air tasking order
The authority to independently negotiate with adversaries

questions.question: In joint operations, the JFACC's recommended apportionment of air sorties is typically expressed as what?
options: A fixed number of aircraft assigned to each component
A set of specific target coordinates
A percentage or priority of total air effort dedicated to various operations such as counterair, air interdiction, and close air support
A mandatory allocation of airframes by aircraft type

questions.question: Expeditionary operations require Air Force units to have what key capability?
options: Permanent basing agreements in every potential area of operations
The ability to maintain full wartime operations only from home stations
Unlimited logistics resources pre-positioned worldwide
The ability to rapidly deploy, employ, sustain, and redeploy forces to meet combatant commander requirements anywhere in the world

questions.question: Which statement best describes the relationship between air superiority and other joint force operations?
options: Air superiority is a prerequisite that enables freedom of action for friendly air, land, and maritime forces while denying the same to the enemy
Air superiority is only relevant during the initial phase of a conflict
Air superiority applies exclusively to manned fighter aircraft operations
Air superiority is automatically achieved when a joint force has numerical advantage in aircraft

questions.question: Multi-capable airmen (MCA) are a key enabler for which operational concept?
options: Strategic nuclear deterrence
Agile Combat Employment (ACE)
Large-scale combat operations from main operating bases
Space launch operations

questions.question: The direct air support center (DASC) is a component of the theater air control system that provides what function?
options: Developing the air tasking order at the operational level
Conducting strategic intelligence assessments
Controlling and directing close air support and other direct air support missions in coordination with ground forces
Managing space operations for the JFACC

questions.question: A tactical air control party (TACP) is attached to ground maneuver units to provide what capability?
options: Maintenance support for deployed aircraft
Logistics planning for ground force resupply
Medical evacuation coordination for the ground commander
Terminal attack control and coordination of air support with the ground commander's scheme of maneuver
